title: practical english usage - Part 318
content:
Feel is often used to talk about reactions and opinions. Progressive forms are not 5 reactions       and   opinions I felt like / as if I was swimming. (= It seemed as if I was swimming.) I felt like swimming. (= I wanted to swim.) Compare: I felt like laughing, but I didn't dare.

In this sense, feel like is often followed by an -ing form.

I feel like a drink. Have you got any beer?

Feel like can also mean 'want', 'would like’ .

4 feel     like    meaning       'want' is also possible, ►    378.) Alice felt as if/though she was in a very nice dream. (Alice felt like she was ...

My legs feel like cotton wool.

Feel can be followed by like or as if/ihough.

3  linking     verb:   feel     like;   feel     as   if/though That feels nice!  The glass felt cold against my lips.

are not used.

Feel can also be used to say that something causes sensations. Progressive forms 2 linking     verb:   That     feels     nice!

How do you feel? / How are you feeling?

I feel fine. / I’ m  feeling fine.

progressive forms can be used. There is little difference of meaning.

To  talk about feelings that are going on at a particular moment, simple or himself inferior...) He always felt inferior when he was with her. (More natural than He always felt In this sense feel is not normally used with reflexive pronouns (myself, etc).

When Louise realised what she had done, she felt a complete idiot. (BrE) Andrew was beginning to feel cold.

I feel fine.  Do you feel happy?

(in British English) noun complements are used.

Feel can be used to talk about one’ s physical or mental sensations. Adjective or 1  linking     verb:   I feel     fine.

a direct object.

an adjective or noun complement. It can also be an ordinary verb, followed by meanings, but not with others. Feel can be a linking verb (►   11), followed by Feel has several different meanings. Progressive forms can be used with some 463 feel Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 vocabulary •  464 female and feminine; male and masculine When at last they found him  he was almost dead.

James has paid me that money at last.

resulting from a long wait or delay.

At last also suggests - very strongly - the idea of impatience or inconvenience 2  at   last we must compete in world markets.

We must increase productivity. We must reduce unemployment. And finally, Finally can also introduce the last element in a series, like lastly (►   284.1).

Josh has finally found a job.

After trying three times, she finally managed to pass her exam.

In this sense, it often goes in mid-position (with the verb, ►    200).

Finally can suggest that one has been waiting a long time for something.

465 finally,       at   last,    in   the    end     and   at   the    end The word for 'moon' is feminine in French and masculine in German.

Feminine and masculine are used for grammatical forms in some languages.

She has a very masculine laugh. It was a very feminine bathroom.

typical of men or women.

Feminine and masculine are used for qualities and behaviour that are felt to be A female fox is called a vixen.  A male duck is called a drake.

Female and male refer to the sex of people, animals and plants.

464 f emale       and   feminine;         male      and   masculine ‘ What are you doing? ’ I'm feeling the shirts to see if they're dry.' Feel the photocopier. It's very hot.

about it or experience it’ . Progressive forms are possible.

Feel can also be used with a direct object to mean ‘ touch something to learn 7 'touch' I can feel something biting me!

sensation that is going on at a particular moment.

Progressive forms are not used, but we  often use can feel to talk about a I suddenly felt an insect crawling up my leg.

come  to us through the sense of touch.

Feel can be used with a direct object to talk about the physical sensations that 6 'receive     physical      sensations' I felt it (to be) my duty to call the police.

We felt it necessary to call the police.

There is also a structure feel it (+ to be) + adjective/noun.

I felt her to be unfriendly. (More normal: I felt that she was unfriendly.) is not very often used.
